characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: Aerith and Bob: Jerry, Elaine, George, and...Cosmo. Downplayed in that Cosmo itself is a conventional given name in real life, just not as commonly used. Big, Thin, Short Trio: For the guys, Kramer, Jerry and George respectively. Consistent Clothing Style: Each of the four main characters has distinctive articles of clothing:Jerry always wears long sleeved button-downs and sneakers.Elaine wears long coats and high-end blouses.George favors checkered long-sleeve shirts.Kramer wears short sleeved vintage button-downs with crazy patterns such as leaves or lobsters paired with slacks. In Elaine's words, he dresses like a "hipster doofus". Jerry always wears long sleeved button-downs and sneakers. Elaine wears long coats and high-end blouses.

characters.name: Jerry Seinfeld
characters.description: Accidental Hero: Humorously invoked in "The Van Buren Boys;" the friends of his then-girlfriend treat his going out with her like he's doing her a great big favor, much to his confusion (Ironically, he breaks up with her after finding out that his parents adore her). Afraid of Blood: "The Blood" shows he has some serious hemophobia. When Kramer starts storing his own blood at home, Jerry gets paranoid that he's going to put it in his (Jerry's) fridge. All Jews Are Cheapskates: Averted. He's the only one of the cast explicitly identified as Jewish and he's fairly generous with money. Amicable Exes: He's Elaine's ex-boyfriend but they still hang out and they're still close friends. As Himself: Only more of a jerk and less rich and famous. Doubles as aCreator Cameo.

characters.name: George Costanza
characters.description: Acquired Situational Narcissism: On the rare occasion things turn out well for him or he gets some boost to his confidence, George can turn into a complete blowhard who is even worse to be around. It gets so bad in "The Beard" when he begins wearing a toupee that Elaine has to physically rip it off his head and throw it out the window. Affably Evil: The most amoral member of the group, and while he can just as easily be outwardly unpleasant or faking affability, George has his share of moments where he's genuinely friendly and even well meaning. Allegedly Dateless: Is bald, a bit overweight, often unemployed, and his personality flaws are too long to list. The show draws heavy attention to what a loser he is, yet he had sex with 43 (very attractive) women over the course of the show. Ambiguously Jewish: On the one hand, he has an Italian last name and his father, while played by a Jewish actor, is allegedly a member of the Knights of Columbus (a Catholic organization). On the other hand, his mother fits theJewish Motherstereotype and is played by a Jewish actress, he's played by a Jewish actor and he is the avatar of co-creator Larry David, who is also Jewish. From this, one can assume he's half-Jewish. And Starring: "AndJason Alexanderas George".

characters.name: Elaine Benes
characters.description: Aborted Declaration of Love: Her telling Jerry: 'I've always lovedyou...nited airlines.' Amicable Exes: She's Jerry's ex-girlfriend but they still hang out and they're still close friends. Big Eater: Even more so than Kramer. A surprisingly large number of her plot lines involve her appetite getting her into trouble. Brainy Brunette: Went to the best university of the four (she went to Tufts), apparently has a high enough IQ that George asked her to help him cheat on his IQ test, and generally has the greatest grounding in culture and literature. What she lacks, then, is common sense and self-restraint, same as the rest. Break the Haughty: One episode in particular had her called to task for abusing the authority J. Peterman left her with to legitimately travel the world. That was basically groveling and something continuously tripped her up at the end of each segment.

characters.name: Cosmo Kramer
characters.description: Accidental Misnaming: In "The Package", he mistook Elaine's surname Benes as "Benett". Ironic, since Kramer himself has always beenreferred to by his own surname. Affably Evil: Though like the others Kramer has done quite a few things that are not morally right, he's still a prettyNice Guywho means well despite this. Most of his worst actions are just because he's too ignorant to see what he's doing is wrong. Ambiguously Bi: Besides kissing Jerry in "The Kiss Hello" as a joke, he's also the only character who openly admits to finding some of the same sex attractive without backtracking or trying to play it as a joke, but nothing is ever set in stone. Arbitrarily Large Bank Account: Kramer is unemployed and has no visible source of other income nor is he ever stated to be on welfare yet he never has an issue affording expensive objects like golf clubs, country club memberships or cuban cigars. George even says he "never works but falls ass backwards into money". Based on a True Story: Claimed to beKenny Kramer's, who had the same relationship with Larry David that Cosmo Kramer has with Jerry Seinfeld.

characters.name: Ruthie Cohen
characters.description: The cashier at Monk's Café. She was the secondary character with the most appearances (101 - after her comes Newman, with only48). A Day in the Limelight:"The Gum", when we discover everyone in the group but George knows her (which he eventually gets to do, because she supposedly "stole" him change for $20although he actually used a $10 bill), and featured the two times we see her outside Monk's (once riding a horse, another driving her car). Early-Installment Weirdness: In the pilot Monk's was Pete's and Ruthie's role (plus that of the other Monk's employees, as well as Elaine's) was fulfilled by Claire, a waitress played by Lee Garlington. Living Prop/Satellite Character: Ruthie was the cashier at Monk's, a restaurant where the group happens to be a regular. Otherwise, we know nothing about her other than the group (except George) knew her. Hell, most of the time she wasn't even really a character, but an extra, and we see her only once outside Monk's.

characters.name: Newman
characters.description: Acrofatic: He can be surprisingly quick on his feet when he needs to be as seen when he runs from the farm house in "The Bottle Deposit" or chases after Kramer in "The Label Maker". He's also able to climb a tree "like a ring-tailed lemur" according to Kramer and is described by Jerry as an exceptional tennis player. Affably Evil: When he's not antagonistic, he seems to get along fairly well with the cast, especially Kramer. Almighty Janitor: Played with. Newman likes to treat his job as a postal worker as if he is part of a complex and sinister organisation of almost unlimited authority which has amassed a surprising amount of power and influence throughout modern society. On some occasions, it's implied that this actually is the case; on others, it's just Newman having delusions of grandeur. Badass Boast: "When you control the mail, you control —information!" Berserk Button: Various mail-related issues can set Newman off, from the general stresses of his job to people complaining about junk mail.

characters.name: Frank Costanza
characters.description: Abusive Parents: It's heavily implied that the reason George is so messed up is from having him as a father, with such defining incidents as the deal with Festivus, calling his son "weak" for needing glasses, etc. Ambiguously Jewish: He seems like it, but actually isn't, despite being played by a Jewish actor. The character of Frank is an Italian Catholic, and one episode established him as being a big deal in his chapter of the Knights of Columbus, a Catholic organization. However, his wife is Jewish so maybe it's rubbed off on him over the years. Angrish:Jason Alexanderhas stated that because Jerry Stiller often had trouble remembering his lines, the gibberish he spouted instead made the final cut as the crew thought it was much funnier.Frank:[shouting]You couldn't smooth a silk sheet if you had a hot date with a babe....[trails off mid-sentence, soon speaking again in a calmer voice]I lost my train of thought. Ass Shove: During his brief separation from Estelle, Frank thought Kramer had made a move on her and got into an altercation, which ended in them both slipping and falling with Frank sitting on a statue of corkscrew pasta Kramer had earlier made and needing to see a proctologist. Awful Wedded Life: Frank and Estelle can hardly have a single conversation without it escalating into furious yelling over something petty and ridiculous, yet they've still remained a married couple for at least 40 years.

characters.name: Estelle Costanza
characters.description: Ambiguously Jewish: Her religion and ethnicity are never stated outright, but she's played by a Jewish actress and embodies basically every Jewish stereotype known to humankind. Jewish Complaining: She's very fussy and prone to exaggerated kvetching in the most nasalBorscht Beltaccent Estelle Harris can muster. Jewish Mother: Her ethnicity is not revealed in the show, but she's portrayed by a Jewish actress and acts like a stereotypical Jewish mother.Jason Alexander:There's no way she can be anythingbutJewish.One of the strongest pieces of evidence is that she hates Germans to the point of refusing to ever get in a German-made car. Jewskind of have a good reason for not liking Germans very much, especially ones who were alive when it happened. One of the strongest pieces of evidence is that she hates Germans to the point of refusing to ever get in a German-made car. Jewskind of have a good reason for not liking Germans very much, especially ones who were alive when it happened. Jews Love to Argue: More often than not, she's arguing with her husband about something or other, and in the most offensively stereotypical "Jewish Mother" voice imaginable.

characters.name: Susan Ross
characters.description: Black Comedy:Her death and the gang's reaction to it are the best examples of this in the entire series. It is described later as "restrained jubilation". Bridezilla: Becomes a bit of one as the wedding approaches, what with her refusal to let Elaine be an usher, declaring it to be "out of the question" as though a female usher is an utterly abhorrent and unheard of idea. Butt-Monkey: Big time, easily the biggest one on the show. Susan goes from being a respected NBC executive to getting vomited on, having her father's cabin burn down, finding out her father was gay, losing her job at NBC, and later getting killed by poisonous envelopes. Character Death: Via the toxic glue found in extremely cheap wedding invitations. She and George were expecting about 200 people. Control Freak: Constantly, George is such a doormat when it comes to her.

characters.name: Mr. and Mrs. Ross
characters.description: Feigning Intelligence: Mr. Ross keeps an impressive personal library but his wife asserts he hasn't actually read anything in it. Forced Out of the Closet: After Mr. Ross's cabin burns down, the only surviving item is a box full of letters from John Cheever detailing their secret and passionate love affair. Susan looks through them with George, Jerry, her mother, her brother and her aunt before an irate Mr. Ross walks in on them, angrily confirming his love for the deceased writer and that he considered him a better partner than his wife. Hidden Disdain Reveal: Their contempt for George was always only barely veiled but the ending ofThe Wizardhas the Rosses bluntly tell him that they never liked him and that they held him responsible for Susan's death. Kick the Dog: They start the Susan Ross Foundation as a way to honor their daughter's memory but also as a way to torment George by having him sit on the organization's board of directors, robbing him of his free time and making him watch as they donate Susan's personal fortune, something he would have inherited had their marriage happened, to anyone in order to make sure a dime of it never goes to him. No Full Name Given: Unlike her husband, Mrs. Ross's first name is never said onscreen.

characters.name: Crazy Joe Davola
characters.description: Affably Evil: Davola's always polite and soft-spoken, only raising his voice in his final appearance, and was even able to charm Elaine enough to be her boyfriend for a while. He's also the only antagonist in the show that the cast is horrified of; he is as genuinely affable as he is dangerous. Arc Villain: Sort of set up as one for Season 4, but he disappears after "The Opera" and makes one final cameo in "The Pilot" before never being seen again. Despite seeming like a major threat, he ultimately doesn't have much impact on the season's actual arc of trying to getJerryon the air. Ax-Crazy: Hence why he's calledCrazyJoe Davola, a moniker he very much earns. Blue-and-Orange Morality: Due to his severe anti-social behavior, he has a sense of right and wrong that makes sense to him and only him. Crouching Moron, Hidden Badass: He soundly kicks the asses of several thugs who try to mug him.

characters.name: J. Peterman
characters.description: Based on a Great Big Lie: His autobiography is actually stories that were bought from Kramer and written by Elaine. Benevolent Boss: Subverted. While heseemsan ideal and friendly boss for Elaine, there are many moments where he treats her like crap—butting into her personal life, which he has no right to do, assigning menial tasks to her that aren't her responsibility—fixing his flat tire—firing her over petty things such as her not likingThe English Patient, etc. He doesn't seem outwardly malicious though, just genuinely oblivious to the impropriety of such things. Bunny-Ears Lawyer: However crazy he may be, he's pretty damn good at what he does. The Bus Came Back: Inverted in one episode with Elaine having to visit him in Burma to have him approve one of her lavish purchases on her account. Then later on, played straight as he returns to run the company once again. Cloudcuckoolander: Quite so. One example has him refuse to make his autobiography action-packed as that is already covered in the catalogues and in another he spends a hefty sum on a ancient piece of confectionary.

characters.name: George Steinbrenner
characters.description: Affectionate Parody: His depiction is somewhere between this and aTake That!: the real Steinbrenner was a controversial figure for his at-times ill-advised "hands-on" approach to running the Yankees, and the show wasn't shy about jabbing him by making him a motor-mouthed weirdo prone to odd flights of fancy. The real Steinbrenner was initially somewhat annoyed by the depiction, but reportedly lightened up after watching a few episodes; he apparently came to consider the depiction unflattering but essentially accurate. Attention Deficit... Ooh, Shiny!: Tiny things will distract him from managing the Yankees, like in "The Calzone" when George got him hooked on eating calzones from an Italian restaurant. The moment he took the first bite, he quickly went off track from the meeting he was in and ordered everyone out of the room because he couldn't take his mind off it. By the episode's end, he was so wound up from calzone withdrawal (George had gotten himself banned from the restaurant, so he had to go to a different one farther away), that when he smelled Kramer's clothes that carried the scent of calzones, he went on a mad dash from his office to find Costanza with the alleged calzones. Bad Boss: In the words of George C, "He fires people like it's a bodily function!" He has a soft spot for Costanza, however. The Bore: Whenever he summons George to his office, the meeting will eventually devolve into Steinbrenner rambling endlessly until George quietly slips out of the office. Cloudcuckoolander: His stories go off on tangents, and he rarely listens to whomever he's talking to.

characters.name: David Puddy
characters.description: Berserk Button: He really doesn't like mechanics being referred to as "grease monkeys". Break the Haughty: He and Elaine go to speak with a minister when she gets fed up with his pontificating. When the minister learns that they aren't married, he informs them that they'rebothhell-bound for engaging in premarital sex, leaving Puddy quite stunned at the realization that he has no right lording his supposedly saintly status over her. Bunny-Ears Lawyer: His eccentricities are largely tolerated because he's a very good and more importantly an honest mechanic. Catchphrase: "That's right." Exact Words: Being a New Jersey Devils fan, Puddy likes to paint his face for the games. Elaine is turned off by this and nearly breaks up with him, but he promises her he won't wear facepaint anymore. Instead. he paints his chest to spell out "DEVILS" with other fans.

characters.name: Jackie Chiles
characters.description: AnAmbulance Chaserwho has the misfortune to be Kramer's go-to lawyer, attorney, and counsel. Bunny-Ears Lawyer: He's a quirky fast talker, but he actually possesses a good deal of legal knowledge, and from what we see of his office and practice he seems very successful. He'd be more successful, though, if Kramer wasn't Kramer. Butt-Monkey: To all appearances, Jackie is a genuinely competent lawyer. Unfortuately, his main client is Kramer, who repeatedly humiliates Jackie by taking deals without discussing it with Jackie (or listening to the entire deal) and solicitly and accepting ill-conceived legal advice from a golf caddy (again, ignoring Jackie's advice). Department of Redundancy Department: The last word of most of his sentences is often repeated, restated, reiterated. Frivolous Lawsuit: Aids Kramer in a number of these, such as suing a coffee company becausethe coffee was too hot. In particular, he was very, very eager to "get a piece" of the tobacco industry. These lawsuits always wind up publicly humiliating him due to Kramer's ignorance and impulsivity.

characters.name: Kenny Bania
characters.description: The Friend Nobody Likes: Jerry only ever hangs with him because he can't avoid him. They perform at the same clubs, and Bania ropes Jerry into owing him favors. Oblivious to Hatred: He's completely unaware that Jerry feels nothing but contempt for him. Sitcom Arch-Nemesis: Though not to the same extent as Newman, due to the hatred being one sided on Jerry's part, everytime he appears in an episode it's to drive Jerry fuming with annoyance. Small Name, Big Ego: He loves to brag about his progress at the gym, and his comedy career when it's going well.Bania:I'm gonna go pick up some chicks. Good-lookin' ones, too! Trademark Favorite Food: He won't stop talking about how great the swordfish at Mendy's is, though he has also said the pea soup and duck there is "the best."

characters.name: Sue Ellen Mischke
characters.description: Hope Spot: She and Elaine almost became actual friends. Sue Ellen was getting married in India, but Elaine thought she only got an invitation as a ploy for a gift and wasn't actually wanted there. She flew out to India to spite her, but Sue Ellen actually greeted her arrival warmly—saying no one else was willing to fly out. Elaine was made maid of honor and all was well... until Sue Ellen found out Elaine slept with the groom a couple years ago. I Have Boobs, You Must Obey!: Jerry is much more sympathetic to her story after she approaches him wearing only a bra. Ms. Fanservice: She intentionally invokes this by wearing a bra as a top. Old Money: Heiress to the O'Henry candy bar fortune. Passive Aggressive Combat: With Elaine. Elaine buys her a bra as she often goes without, and the next time they meet Sue Ellen is wearing it as a top. She also tends to make some snide comments regarding the disparity in their income.

characters.name: Justin Pitt
characters.description: Bad Boss: Makes Elaine do trivial things such as taking the salt off his pretzels and finding the perfect brand of socks for him to wear. British Stuffiness: He is posh and very fussy about his likes and dislikes, often to the point of absurdity. Cloudcuckoolander: He eats Snickers with a knife and fork. Enmity with an Object: Only allows pencils as he hates ink to the point of having it banned in his office. Jerk with a Heart of Gold: In spite of his demanding nature and the menial work he has Elaine do, he thought of her as a daughter and almost put her in his will, but that ended after he thoughtElaine and Jerry tried to kill himand promptly fired her.

characters.name: Mickey Abbott
characters.description: Berserk Button: Don't call him a midget; he prefers the term "little person". Big Guy, Little Guy: The little guy to Kramer's big guy, and is often the brains behind whatever he is doing with Kramer. Category Traitor: When he wears lifts in his shoes to keep a role, the other dwarf actors find out and he's all but ostracized for it. The Napoleon: What he lacks in stature, he more than makes up for it with his temper. Serial Spouse: Is revealed in one episode to having tied the knot five times, and marries his sixth wife in the same one. He also calls a woman whosesecondmarriage just ended a "lightweight".

characters.name: Morty Seinfeld
characters.description: Enmity with an Object: Does not like Velcro, much to the point he threw away a wallet Jerry gave him as a gift because it had a Velcro strip. Grumpy Old Man: He is definitely a grouch in his senior years. Happily Married: Downplayed but he and Helen definitely have a much happier and healthier relationship than the Costanzas. Papa Wolf: Willing to stick up for Jerry, even if Jerry finds it unnecessary ("The Pen" comes to mind.) Took a Level in Jerkass: Becomes more cantankerous once Barney Martin took over the role.

characters.name: Uncle Leo
characters.description: Alter Kocker: He's the stereotype of an old, grumpy Jew. The Bore: Jerry finds dealing with him incredibly tiresome. Catchphrase: The way he says "Jerry!" and "Hello!", much to Jerry's annoyance. In "The Bookstore", Uncle Leo even has "JERRY" and "HELLO"tattooed onto his fingersduring Jerry's nightmare sequence. Characterization Marches On: In his first apperance in "The Pony Remark", Uncle Leo, while still a pest towards Jerry, is more of a stern, no-nonsense blowhard as opposed to his eventual characterization of a nutjob whoaccuses everyone of anti-semitism. Everything Is Racist: ARunning Gaghas him blaming everything that inconveniences him on anti-Semitism, including petty things like a hamburguer being slightly more well-done than he ordered.Leo:Look at this! I told them medium rare, it's medium!Jerry:Hey, it happens.Leo:I bet that cook is an anti-Semite!Jerry:He can't even see you! He has no idea who you are!Leo:They don'tjust overcooka hamburger, Jerry.Jerry:Fine. Anyway, the point I was making beforeGoebbelsmade your hamburger here is this...

characters.name: Russell Dalrymple
characters.description: Love Makes You Crazy: Became obsessively infatuated with Elaine, which eventually led to him leaving NBC and, ultimately,his demise. No Celebrities Were Harmed: Is partly based on real-life NBC executive Warren Littlefield (who Balaban coincidentally played in the HBO adaptation ofThe Late Shift). Mean Boss: He fired an employee just because of the way he served him a cup of coffee. He laters fires another guy forabsolutely no reasonjust because he's pissed off about Elaine rejecting him and decides to take it out on someone else.

characters.name: Tim Whatley
characters.description: Depraved Dentist: He does several things that no decent dentist should ever do. He doesn't allow kids in his office, keeps copies of Penthouse in the lobby, takes hits of nitrous oxide before beginning procedures, and routinely swaps nurses with his fellow dentists, and at one point, Jerry finds out that he was violated during an anesthesia-induced coma. He also does a painful procedure on Jerry after he makes a joke comparing dentists to sadists. Jewish Smartass: Converts to Judaism in "The Yada Yada". Jerry believes he's just doing it for the jokes. Love Interest: He dated Elaine briefly. N-Word Privileges: Jerry claims that Tim became Jewish just to get away with making jokes about Judaism.

characters.name: The Soup Nazi
characters.description: Alternate Character Interpretation:In-Universe, Kramer sees him as a man who simply wants toserve his soup quickly and efficientlyand wants customers to respect this goal. Argentina Is Nazi-Land: Appropriately, the Soup Nazi flees to Argentina after being "defeated" in his debut episode. Asshole Victim: His arrogance and abrasive personality make it hard not to enjoy Elaine's revenge on him. Berserk Button:Manyincluding but not limited to changing orders midway, not having money ready, stalling, making small talk, not knowing what you want in advance, kissing in line, not speaking clearly, haggling over prices, asking for free bread, complaining about anything, speaking Spanish andtelling himhe looks likeAl Pacino. Bunny-Ears Lawyer: Sure, he's incredibly strict, and will refuse service at the drop of a hat, but he still gets customers around the block because his soup isjust that good.

characters.name: Alton Benes
characters.description: A published author who is the father of Elaine. The Alcoholic: Elaine jokes that alcohol is her father's religion and that his ethnicity is "half-drunk." The Comically Serious: He's always dead serious and intense and reacts badly to attempts at humor:Alton:Which one's supposed to be the funny guy?George:Oh! He's the comedian.Jerry:I-I'm just a regular person.George:Nah, nah. He's just being modest.Alton:We had a funny guy with us in Korea. Tailgunner. They blew his brains out all over the Pacific.[beat]There's nothin' funny aboutthat. The Dreaded: Both Jerry and George are flat out terrified of him.George:We can't possibly have dinner with him alone!Jerry:How are we going to get out of this?George:We say we're frightened and have to go home!This was the case behind the scenes as well, given Tierney was an infamously volatile actor from whom no one knew what to expect and as such scared the shit out of cast and crew alike during filming. It got to the point that writers would jokingly threaten to bring Tierney back when having a dispute with the actors. This was the case behind the scenes as well, given Tierney was an infamously volatile actor from whom no one knew what to expect and as such scared the shit out of cast and crew alike during filming. It got to the point that writers would jokingly threaten to bring Tierney back when having a dispute with the actors.

characters.name: Bob Sacamano
characters.description: Born Unlucky: If Kramer's stories are to be believed, Bob has went through botched surgery, rabies, and shock treatment at a mental hospital. The Ghost: Kramer constantly brings up Bob Sacamano's misadventures, but Bob himself never appears onscreen. Unlike other friends of Kramer, however, Bob isn't a ghost to the other main characters. He invites the group to a party in "The Fatigues," and Jerry befriends Bob when he takes over Kramer's apartment in "The Chicken Roaster." Iron Butt-Monkey: Despite everything he's been through he never seems to die. Particularly impressive considering rabies has a 99% fatality rate once symptoms appear, even with modern treatments. New Job as the Plot Demands: Bob appears to have had several sources of income. In the episode "The Fix-Up," his new job at a condom factory helps set up the plot.

characters.name: Lt. Joe Bookman
characters.description: Cold Ham: He manages to chew the scenery without raising his voice. The Comically Serious: Takes overdue library books and the fines associated with them very seriously, asking Jerry at one pointif he's ever killed somebody. Expy: His deadpan, no-nonsense demeanor with his rapid fire monologue delivery and interrogation of Jerry are obvious references to Joe Friday fromDragnet. Good Old Ways: Pines for the old days when librarians were older, kindly, and had no private life. Inspector Javert: Grills Jerry on his grossly-overdue library book, correctly asserting that he never returned it despite Jerry's protests.

characters.name: Donald "Bubble Boy" Sanger
characters.description: Asshole Victim: Even though his plastic bubble got popped by Susan, he was such a douche that the audience can't help but feel relief to his predicament. Disability as an Excuse for Jerkassery: Played with. He has to live in a plastic bubble due to his low immune system, yet he's constantly an asshole to George and Susan, as well as to his own parents. When Georgefights back, the neighbors become outraged: "What kind of person would hurt the Bubble Boy?" However, the Bubble Boy doesn't even try to use his disability to his advantage in order to get people to excuse his behavior; people just feel bad for him anyway. Disproportionate Retribution: He attacks George simply because he refuses to admit that the answer to the Trivial Pursuit question is "Moors", not "Moops". Establishing Character Moment: The viewers learn immediately that he is an awful person the first time they hear his voice, as he screams down to his mother demanding she bring him food and threatens her with violence if she doesn't give him what he wants. Freudian Excuse: Given that he's stuck in a tiny plastic bubble for his entire life, it's not hard to see why he's so ill-tempered and abrasive.

characters.name: Babu Bhatt
characters.description: Butt-Monkey: Ironically, despite Jerry attempting to genuinely help him every time he shows up, his life gets ruined even more than it already was. Misplaced Retribution: He swears revenge on Jerry for getting him deported, when the only thing Jerry was guilty of was being out of town during the weeks Babu's visa was mistakenly put in his mailbox. Never My Fault: He blames his failures on Jerry, when most of them were caused by Babu being a terrible businessman or just bad luck. Even Jerry attempting to help and support him does nothing to change his mind. Stereotypical South Asian English: He speaks with an exaggerated singsong tone of voice, says "very" a lot, and also wags his index finger back and forth when speaking.

characters.name: Lloyd Braun
characters.description: Always Someone Better: He's usually better than George at something, and George's parents are quick to tell George that. Played with, as he’s actually an incompetent wreck who is even more of a messed-up failure than George; he’s just better at hiding it. Ax-Crazy: According to George he was institutionalized after they found a family in his freezer. Break the Haughty: He’s insufferably smug until he tanks Mayor Dinkins’ election with the name tag idea he stole from Elaine, destroying his own reputation. Laser-Guided Karma: He steals Elaine’s nametag idea and passes it off as his own, hoping to use it to get Mayor Dinkins re-elected. The nametag idea proves to be a disaster that causes Rudy Giuliani to win the election and the fallout ruins Lloyd’s life and causes him to have a nervous breakdown. Sanity Slippage: After his life is ruined, he suffers a mental breakdown and is left a paranoid, incompetent wreck. His final appearance heavily implies he murdered a family and stuffed them in his freezer.

characters.name: Sidra Holland
characters.description: Back for the Finale: She's one of the minor characters to return for the finale to testify against Jerry and Elaine for the groping incident at the sauna, and ends up hooking up with Jackie Chiles just to pour salt on the wound for Jerry. Big-Breast Pride: She refers to her own breasts as beingspectacular. Buxom Beauty Standard: Her breasts are apparently so big and "perfect" that Elaine becomes convinced that they're fake and she's aPlastic Bitch.Elaine:C'mon, don't you think they seem a bit too perfect?Jerry:Yes, they do! Girl of the Week: Was Jerry'sLove Interestfor "The Implant." Informed Attribute: While her breasts are sizable, they're not the mammoths the characters treat them as.

characters.name: Rachel Goldstein
characters.description: Accidental Pervert: In "The Hamptons", she ends up walking in on George naked, and right when he was out of the cold pool, meaning he had aTeeny Weeniedue to shrinkage,much to his horror. Against My Religion: As a kosher, her religion forbids her to eat lobster, so George takes revenge on her by secretly feeding scrambled eggs with lobster,much to her horror. Disproportionate Retribution: A victim of this due to George'sInsane Troll Logic. First, he wants to see her naked due to Jerry seeing George's girlfriend,Jane, topless on the beach. He even attempts to walk in on her while she's getting dressed "by accident". Ironically, she ends up walking on him naked, discovering he has aTeeny Weenieand ends up telling Jane about it. In revenge forthatGeorge serves her scrambled eggs without telling her they contain lobster, which isagainst her religion. Love Interest: Of all of Jerry's girlfriends, she's the one that lasted the longest, in a total of 4 episodes. Parental Marriage Veto: Her kosher father greatly dislikes Jerry due to the fact he made out with her in a public cinema while watchingSchindler's List, and tries to forbid their relationship. But she and Jerry still keep dating anyway, and JerryHand Wavesit by telling Elaine he bought her dad some kishka.

characters.name: Jeannie Steinman
characters.description: Distaff Counterpart: To Jerry. To the point that during theirFalling-in-Love Montage, she can be seen reading from aSupergirlcomic book, while he's reading from aSupermanone (remember that his love of Superman has been well established throughout the show)

characters.name: Dolores
characters.description: Accent Depundent:In-Universe. Dolores only rhymes withclitorisif you put the stress on the second syllable (cli-TOR-is), whereas the standard pronounciation stresses thefirstsyllable (CLIT-oris), which may be why Jerry and George had such a hard time figuring it out. The Bus Came Back: Dolores makes a brief reappearance 4 seasons later in "The Foundation" where she became interested in Jerry again after being impressed that he went as far as getting engaged to Jeannie Steinman. However, the attempt to restart their relationship doesn't last long as she finds it impossible to believe that Jerry and Jeannie somehow had a completely mutual breakup, and just views it as Jerry still being a child who hasn't grown up. Girl of the Week: Was Jerry'sLove Interestfor "The Junior Mint." In-Series Nickname: In an attempt to figure out what Dolores' name is, Jerry and George guess a bunch of names that rhyme with a female body part since it was the only hint they were given. The one that stuck the most was "Mulva." Late-Arrival Spoiler: "The Junior Mint" keeps her name secret even through the end credits, where she's billed as "Mystery woman". Jerry lands on the right namejustbefore the vanity card (not that it matters, since she dumped him). "The Foundation" establishes her name from the get-go.

characters.name: Gary Fogel
characters.description: Catchphrase: "Good for you, Jack!" Killed Offscreen: Shortly after his sole episode appearance, another episode reveals he's died in a car accident while trying to fix his toupee. Playing Sick: Fakes having cancer so he can get a toupee from Jerry tocover up his baldness.
